coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over coal-based carbon quantum dots modified CoMoO4/g-C3N4

coredata.dc:description: In pursuit of exploring functional applications of coal, this study ultrasound-assisted hydrogen peroxide was used to transform cheap coal into environmentally friendly and high-value coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). These CQDs are then harnessed to synergistically assist the photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen evolution, in collaboration with CoMoO4 (CM) and g-C3N4 (CN). The results indicated that the novel composite photocatalyst, CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, exhibited high hydrogen evolution activity, reaching a maximum hydrogen production rate of 4916.63 μmol g−1 h−1 at pH 9. At a wavelength of 420 nm, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was 1.78%. The conduction and valence band positions of CM were determined to be −1.03 eV and 1.65 eV, respectively, while those of CN were −0.83 eV and 1.72 eV, suggesting the formation of an S-type heterojunction between CM and CN. The significant role of SCQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was extensively investigated, demonstrating that the synergistic effect between SCQDs, CM, and CN could enhance the hydrogen evolution activity. SCQDs exhibited excellent electron transfer capabilities, serving as bridges for electron transport, functioning both as electron acceptors and donors, effectively impeding the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing their separation efficiency. This study provides crucial insights into the utilization of coal-based CQDs for photocatalytic water splitting, offering an economically viable and sustainable option for the functional application of coal and opening new avenues for the rational design of efficient and cost-effective photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over coal-based carbon quantum dots modified CoMoO4/g-C3N4

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In pursuit of exploring functional applications of coal, this study ultrasound-assisted hydrogen peroxide was used to transform cheap coal into environmentally friendly and high-value coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). These CQDs are then harnessed to synergistically assist the photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen evolution, in collaboration with CoMoO4 (CM) and g-C3N4 (CN). The results indicated that the novel composite photocatalyst, CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, exhibited high hydrogen evolution activity, reaching a maximum hydrogen production rate of 4916.63 μmol g−1 h−1 at pH 9. At a wavelength of 420 nm, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was 1.78%. The conduction and valence band positions of CM were determined to be −1.03 eV and 1.65 eV, respectively, while those of CN were −0.83 eV and 1.72 eV, suggesting the formation of an S-type heterojunction between CM and CN. The significant role of SCQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was extensively investigated, demonstrating that the synergistic effect between SCQDs, CM, and CN could enhance the hydrogen evolution activity. SCQDs exhibited excellent electron transfer capabilities, serving as bridges for electron transport, functioning both as electron acceptors and donors, effectively impeding the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing their separation efficiency. This study provides crucial insights into the utilization of coal-based CQDs for photocatalytic water splitting, offering an economically viable and sustainable option for the functional application of coal and opening new avenues for the rational design of efficient and cost-effective photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: With the escalating consumption of fossil fuels such as coal and petroleum, environmental pollution issues have become increasingly prominent. In order to meet the daily needs of human life, there is an urgent demand for renewable and clean energy sources [1]. Hydrogen gas possesses advantages such as high calorific value, lack of pollution, and renewability. There are numerous methods for hydrogen production, and many experts and scholars are devoted to photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production utilizing solar energy [2,3]. The reason for this is that solar energy can be transformed into clean hydrogen energy, making the entire process environmentally friendly and pollution-free [4,5]. Currently, the focal point of research in photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production lies in the development of high-performance and inexpensive catalysts [6].

g-C3N4 is a representative non-metallic polymer semiconductor characterized by its low cost and simple synthesis methods [7]. Controlled morphological tuning can be achieved by adjusting the sintering temperature during fabrication or through pre-treatment conditions. The inherent morphology of g-C3N4 is block-like, while modified forms include sheet-like and tubular structures. Nanosheets of g-C3N4 exhibit a larger specific surface area and pore volume, providing abundant active sites for hydrogen evolution reactions, shortening charge transfer pathways, and enhancing the redox capability of photo-generated charge carriers [8]. g-C3N4 serves as an excellent host and is often used in conjunction with noble metal catalysts, significantly boosting the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity [9]. Despite the excellent performance of noble metal catalysts, their high cost and limited availability make them unsuitable for large-scale deployment.

Transition metals are abundant on Earth, and transition metal oxides are characterized by their affordability, simple synthesis methods, and excellent redox properties, making them popular in photocatalytic applications [10]. Commonly used transition metal elements include Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn. Co-based bimetallic oxides offer advantages such as abundant active sites, good conductivity, high stability, and excellent catalytic performance. Liu [11] et al. combined Co2NiO4 with NiWO4 to form a p-n heterojunction, and the optimized CN/NW-3 composite achieved a hydrogen production rate of 341.83 μmol in 5 h, 19 times higher than pure Co2NiO4. He [12] et al. prepared CoFe2O4 and NiFe2O4 photocatalysts. TEOA and methanol were chosen as sacrificial agents, and when TEOA was used, the hydrogen generation rates of CoFe2O4 and NiFe2O4 were 0.088 mmol·g−1 h−1 and 0.026 mmol·g−1 h−1, respectively. Bimetallic oxide catalysts with a Co base generally exhibit good catalytic activity. Among them, CoMoO4 stands out due to its stability, low cost, favorable optical and electrochemical properties [13], and it has been widely applied in electrocatalytic hydrogen production [14]. However, its use in photocatalytic hydrogen production remains relatively limited.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of nanomaterials with diameters smaller than 10 nm and quasi-spherical structures [15]. Common carbon sources for CQD synthesis include glucose, citric acid, amino acids, urea, coal, and biomass. Coal is widely distributed, inexpensive, and resource-rich, making coal-based CQDs a low-cost option with excellent photochemical stability [16]. CQDs are easily modifiable and tunable [17], and S doping widens their visible light absorption range, improves electron transfer, optimizes their electronic structure, and increases the reactivity centers due to the slight difference in electronegativity between carbon and sulfur, making C–S bonding challenging. Chemical sulfur doping of CQDs is difficult, and therefore, SCQDs have been seldom used in the field of photocatalysis. Additionally, CQDs can effectively hinder the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs, enhancing their separation efficiency [18]. Based on these outstanding characteristics, CQDs are commonly employed in the field of photocatalysis to modify semiconductor catalysts and enhance their catalytic performance. Li [19] et al. synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated 3D ordered macroporous (3DOM) SrTiO3–TiO2 using polystyrene (PS) colloidal spheres as templates. The prepared CQDs/3DOM SrTiO3–TiO2 exhibited a hydrogen evolution rate of 578.95 μmol g−1 after 8 h, which was 14.5 times higher than that of TiO2 alone. Chen [20] et al. prepared carbon quantum dot/covalent triazine framework (CQDs/CTF) catalysts via an impregnation method for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. With the addition of 0.24% CQDs, the hydrogen evolution activity of the catalyst increased threefold, with a hydrogen evolution rate of 102 μmol·g−1 h−1 compared to the original CTF-1 activity of 34.5 μmol g−1 h−1. Zhao [21] et al. synthesized CQDs using sodium citrate and ammonium bicarbonate as carbon sources via a hydrothermal method and combined them with TiO2 (P25) for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The results indicated that the addition of CQDs enhanced the hydrogen evolution rate. The hydrogen evolution rate of P25-CQDs-H reached 1.42 mmol h−1 g−1, which was seven times higher than pure P25 (0.20 mmol h−1 g−1). Qu [22] et al. used l-cysteine as a carbon source to synthesize N,S-CQDs through a hydrothermal method and then combined them with ZnO for photocatalytic degradation. After the addition of N,S-CQDs, the catalytic efficiency of ZnO/N,S-CQDs for degrading MB, RhB, MG, and antibiotics showed significant improvement compared to ZnO alone. Currently, most CQD synthesis methods utilize organic small molecules as carbon sources and apply them as co-catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution or degradation studies. However, there is limited research on the use of coal-based CQDs as co-catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

CoMoO4 exhibits good stability and low cost, and this study employs it for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. However, when used alone, CoMoO4 displays low activity and suboptimal catalytic effects. In this research, a combination of CoMoO4 nanorods and g-C3N4 nanosheets was used, and SCQDs were introduced to modify the semiconductor catalyst to enhance its catalytic performance. Due to the small electronegativity difference between sulfur and carbon, forming C–S bonds is challenging. Chemically introducing sulfur doping into carbon quantum dots is highly difficult, but high-sulfur coal offers favorable natural conditions for in-situ synthesis of SCQDs. Therefore, this study utilized high-sulfur coal from Lingshi, Shanxi, as a carbon source and employed ultrasonic-assisted hydrogen peroxide treatment to achieve in-situ sulfur doping and prepare SCQDs. These SCQDs were used to modify CoMoO4 and g-C3N4, investigating their performance in photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen evolution.

The coal samples used in the experiments were Ningdong Renjiazhuang bituminous coal (RJZ) and Lingshi bituminous coal from Shanxi (LS). The reagents used included urea (CO(NH2)2), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), Eosin Y (C20H6Br4Na2O5), triethanolamine (C6H15NO3), and anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), all purchased from Aladdin. All reagents used were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proximate and ultimate analysis of coal samples and sulfur forms analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The coal samples were crushed and sieved to obtain particles with a size smaller than 74 μm. According to GB/T 212–2008, the proximate analysis of coal samples was conducted. The forms of sulfur in the coal samples were analyzed according to GB/T 214–2007. Table 1 shows the results of proximate and ultimate analysis of coal samples and sulfur forms analysis. Low-sulfur coal RJZ was used for synthesizing CQDs, while high-sulfur coal LS was employed for synthesizing SCQDs.

Preparation of g-C3N4 Nanosheets: g-C3N4 nanosheets were synthesized using a thermal condensation method with urea as the precursor. 10 g of urea was placed in a corundum crucible, covered with a crucible lid, and placed in a muffle furnace. The temperature was ramped up to 600 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min, and the mixture was kept at this temperature for 4 h. After cooling, a light yellow powder was obtained, which was transferred to a beaker, followed by the addition of deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min, then filtered and washed. The obtained material was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h, resulting in g-C3N4 nanosheets designated as CN.

Preparation of CoMoO4: For the synthesis of CoMoO4, 1.46 g of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and 0.88 g of sodium molybdate dihydrate were individually weighed. A total of 60 mL of deionized water was taken and added to a beaker, followed by thorough stirring. The mixture was then transferred to a reaction vessel and maintained at 100 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid sample was collected through filtration and subsequently dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h. Upon drying, the sample was ground and collected. The sample was placed in a muffle furnace, and the furnace temperature was set to 500 °C, with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, and held at this temperature for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was collected and identified as CoMoO4, designated as CM.

Preparation of CQDs: For the synthesis of CQDs, 0.3 g of raw coal was weighed and placed in a beaker. Then, 20 mL of H2O2 and 30 mL of H2O were added to the beaker, followed by ultrasonication to disperse and mix the solution for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a magnetic stirrer and stirred at 40 °C for 3 h. The temperature was then adjusted to 95 °C to decompose excess H2O2. A glass rod was used to transfer a small amount of liquid onto starch-iodine paper. If the paper turned blue, it indicated the presence of H2O2; if it remained unchanged, it indicated complete decomposition of H2O2. After cooling, the mixture was vacuum filtered, and the filtrate was placed in a 1 kDa dialysis bag for 24 h. The dialyzed liquid was concentrated to 5 mL and then freeze-dried at −40 °C to obtain carbon quantum dots powder. CQDs were prepared using low-sulfur coal RJZ, which has a low sulfur content, while SCQDs were prepared using high-sulfur coal LS with a high sulfur content.

Preparation of CoMoO4/g-C3N4/SCQDs: Following hydrothermal treatment, the partially calcined CoMoO4 precursor and g-C3N4 were weighed in proportion. Then, 30 mL of H2O was added, and the mixture was placed in a glass beaker and subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. The mixture was then vacuum filtered, dried, and transferred to a muffle furnace for sintering. The furnace temperature was set to 500 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, and the sample was held at this temperature for 3 h. After natural cooling, the collected sample was labeled as CMCN-xy (x and y represent the mass ratio of CM to CN). CMCN-xy and SCQDs were weighed in proportion and placed together in a 50 mL glass beaker. Then, 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added, and the mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min. The beaker was transferred to a 75 °C water bath, and the solution was stirred while heating in the water bath until the liquid in the beaker evaporated. The obtained sample was collected, dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h, resulting in the composite catalyst labeled as CMCN-xy/z%SCQDs (z% represents the mass percentage of SCQDs in relation to CMCN) (see Fig. 1 ).

X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis of the sample's crystal structure was conducted using a Rigaku RINT-2000 X-ray diffractometer with a working current of 40 mA and a working voltage of 40 kV. A Fourier infrared spectrometer (Nicolet Avatar 380) from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA, was used to analyze the surface functional groups. The samples were prepared using the press method, and the KBr was homogeneously mixed with the sample at a ratio of 100:1, and scanned 32 times in the scan range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Morphological observations were performed using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) with an FEI Talos F200S instrument operating at 200 kV. Some morphological analyses were carried out using a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma 300). Before imaging, the samples were coated with a thin gold layer using a Quorum SC7620 sputter coater for 45 s, and the imaging was conducted at an accelerating voltage of 3 kV. The changes in the specific surface area and pore structure of the samples were characterized using a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 surface area and porosity analyzer (BET) at 150 °C under vacuum for 6 h. The elemental composition and binding energy changes of the catalyst were characterized using an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) instrument, specifically the Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi + XPS spectrometer. Steady-state and transient fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer, with a xenon lamp as the light source. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were analyzed using a TU-1950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer from Beijing PuXi General Instrument Co., Ltd., with a scanning interval of 1 nm, spectral bandwidth of 2.0 nm, response time of 1.0 s, and light sources including xenon and tungsten lamps. Zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Zeta potential analyzer (LITESIZER 500), with the temperature set at 25 °C and an equilibration time of 1 min.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiments were conducted in quartz bottles with a volume of 65 mL. In each quartz bottle, 10 mg of catalyst and 20 mg of Eosin Y dye were added, followed by the addition of 30 mL of a 10% aqueous solution of triethanolamine (TEOA). After 10 min of ultrasonication, the bottles were purged with nitrogen (N2) for 10 min to remove impurities from the gas phase within the bottles. The experiments were carried out in a multi-channel photochemical reaction apparatus with a 5 W LED light source (λ ≥ 420 nm). Magnetic stirring was employed during the reaction to ensure even dispersion of the catalyst. The reaction lasted for 5 h, and samples were taken every 1 h, with 0.5 mL of gas extracted. The amount of generated hydrogen gas was determined using gas chromatography (Tianmei GC7900, TCD, 13Xcolumn, N2 as the carrier gas). Four consecutive cycle stability tests were performed under the same conditions. After each cycle, the reaction vessel was purged with gas to maintain an anaerobic environment. Apparent Quantum Efficiency (AQE) measurements were conducted using a 300 W xenon lamp as the light source and bandpass filters with different wavelengths (420, 475, 500, 520, and 550 nm) to determine AQE values at different wavelengths.

The photoelectrochemical characteristics of different catalysts were tested using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation manufactured by Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd. Transient photocurrent responses, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (MS) measurements were performed on working electrodes coated with catalysts. In the standard three-electrode cell configuration, a 0.2 mol L−1 Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte for photoelectrochemical testing. The auxiliary electrode was a platinum metal piece, and the reference electrode was Hg/HgO. The working electrode was an ITO (indium tin oxide) conductive glass coated with a mixed solution of the photocatalyst. Using 300 W xenon lamp as irradiation source, the effective area of working electrode is 1 × 1 cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure characterization, and composition analysis

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the crystal structure of the catalysts, X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was performed on the prepared catalysts. Fig. 2 presents the XRD patterns of the samples, where Fig. 2(a) shows the XRD patterns of CM, CN, and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. It is observed that the diffraction peaks of the synthesized CM are located at 13.20°, 23.33°, 26.51°, 28.40°, 32.09°, 33.71°, 36.76°, 38.87°, 40.23°, 47.02°, 53.42°, and 54.55°, which correspond to the (001), (021), (002), (−311), (−131), (−222), (400), (040), (003), (241), (−441), and (440) crystal planes of the standard CoMoO4 card (PDF#21–0868), respectively. The diffraction peaks at 12.70° and 27.40° for CN correspond to the (100) and (002) crystal planes [23–25], respectively, which are attributed to the stacking of tri-s-triazine units and graphite layers [26,27]. This confirms the successful synthesis of CN and CM.

Fig. 2(b) presents the XRD test results of composite catalysts with different proportions. It is observed that the composite catalysts exhibit characteristic peaks of both CN and CM, indicating that the composite catalysts are primarily composed of CN and CM. At 26.51°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of CM, the intensity of this characteristic peak increases with the higher proportion of CM, illustrating an enhanced peak intensity. Similarly, at 27.40°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of CN, the intensity of the characteristic peak also increases or decreases in accordance with the varying proportion of CN, further affirming the predominance of CN and CM within CMCN-11/8%SCQDs.

Fig. 3 (a) depicts the FT-IR spectra of CQDs and SCQDs. From Fig. 3(a), it can be observed that the surface functional groups of the prepared CQDs are of the same types, and the surface bears abundant hydrophilic functional groups. Absorption peaks at 3392 cm−1, 2300 cm−1, 1693 cm−1, 1460 cm−1, and 1045 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of –NH and –OH, –COOH, CO, –CH3, and –CH2, and C–O–C, respectively [28,29]. Absorption peaks at 540 cm−1, 1015 cm−1, and 1159 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of S–S, CS, and C–S, respectively [30,31]. The pronounced stretching vibrations of S–S, CS, and C–S in the spectrum of SCQDs confirm the successful preparation of SCQDs and the accomplishment of sulfur in-situ doping.

Fig. 3(b) illustrates the FT-IR spectra of CM, CN, and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. It can be observed that CN's absorption bands at 3000–3400 cm−1 and 1000–1800 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of –NH/-OH and C–N/CN, respectively [32]. An absorption peak at 812 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibrations of the triazine ring of CN [33]. The absorption bands at 960 cm−1 and 645 cm−1 for CM are due to the stretching vibrations of Mo–O and Co–Mo–O bonds [34]. The composite catalyst CMCN-11/8%SCQDs exhibits characteristic absorption peaks of both CN and CM, further confirming the successful synthesis of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs.

Fig. 4 presents the SEM and TEM images of the different samples. In Fig. 4(a), the SEM image of CN reveals a sheet-like structure of the prepared CN. In Fig. 4(b), the TEM image provides a clearer view of the nanosheet structure of CN. Fig. 4(c) displays the SEM image of CM, showing a rod-like structure. The monomer CoMoO4 was irregular rod-shaped, and the ratio of length to diameter was 7:1–12:1. The distribution of CoMoO4 was uniform and showed cross-overlap distribution. In Fig. 4(d), the SEM image of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs indicates the coexistence of nanosheets (CN) on the left and rod-like structures (CM) on the right, confirming the successful composite formation of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The TEM image of CQDs is shown in Fig. 4(e), and the inset displays the particle size distribution of CQDs. Analysis using Nano Measure software reveals a maximum particle size of 5.03 nm, a minimum size of 1.80 nm, and an average size of 2.95 nm for CQDs. Similarly, the TEM image of SCQDs is displayed in Fig. 4(f), with the inset showing the particle size distribution of SCQDs. Analysis indicates a maximum particle size of 4.65 nm, a minimum size of 1.17 nm, and an average size of 3.13 nm for SCQDs. Comparing the particle sizes of CQDs and SCQDs, it is evident that sulfur doping leads to an increase in the size of carbon quantum dots.

Fig. 4(g) displays the TEM image of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, where the transparent region on the edge corresponds to CN nanosheets, while the rod-like structure represents CM. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs is shown in Fig. 4(h), revealing a lattice spacing of 3.36 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of CM. Fig. 4(i) and (j) present element mapping images, illustrating the presence of Co, Mo, O, C, N, and S elements in CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. Specifically, only SCQDs contain the S element, evident from the sulfur presence in the element mapping results. This confirms the successful in-situ sulfur doping of CQDs and the successful introduction of SCQDs into the composite catalyst. Consequently, it indicates the successful preparation of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs.

Fig. 5 (a) displays the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples. From the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms in Fig. 5(a), it is evident that the nitrogen adsorption curve of the samples follows Type IV isotherms with distinct H3 hysteresis loops [35], indicating a predominance of mesopores in the pore size distribution of the prepared CMCN-11/8%SCQDs [36]. Fig. 5(b) shows the pore size distribution curve, revealing that the pore sizes are mainly distributed within the range of less than 50 nm. The adsorption performance parameters of the catalysts in Table 2 indicate that the average pore sizes of CM, CN, CMCN-11, and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs are 10.47, 21.87, 14.98, and 14.27 nm, respectively, further confirming that the pores in the prepared samples are mainly mesoporous. The specific surface areas of CM, CN, CMCN-11, and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs are 9.35, 92.93, 36.15, and 32.38 m2 g⁻1, respectively. By comparison, CN nanosheets exhibit the largest specific surface area. The incorporation of CN into the composite CMCN-11 significantly increases the specific surface area, providing more active sites for the reaction process. The specific surface area of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs is slightly smaller compared to CMCN-11, along with slightly reduced pore volume and pore size, due to the partial filling of pores caused by the introduction of SCQDs. SCQDs can adhere not only to the material surface but also inside the material pores, resulting in a slightly smaller specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size for CMCN-11/8%SCQDs compared to CMCN-11. The introduction of SCQDs provides additional reactive sites for CMCN-11.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted to characterize the elemental composition of carbon quantum dots and catalysts, their chemical composition, and interface interactions. Fig. 6 presents XPS spectra of CQDs and various catalysts. Fig. 6(a) shows the full spectra of CQDs and SCQDs, revealing that SCQDs primarily consist of elements O, N, C, and S. The S peak in the SCQDs spectrum is significantly higher than in CQDs, indicating successful in-situ sulfur doping in SCQDs.

Fig. 6(b) displays the C 1s spectra of CQDs and SCQDs. Three characteristic peaks correspond to electron binding energies of C–C (284.4–284.8) eV, C–O (286.3–287.8) eV, and CO (288.4–289.9) eV [37]. Fig. 6(c) shows the S 2p spectra, where the S 2p peaks in CQDs and SCQDs can be deconvoluted into sulfate sulfur, sulfoxide, sulfone, thiophene, and pyrite sulfur, corresponding to electron binding energy ranges of (168.5–169.5) eV, (167.5–168.5) eV, (165.0–166.0) eV, (164.0–164.4) eV, and (163.6–164.0) eV [38], respectively. Table 3 presents the relative content of different sulfur forms based on peak areas. According to Table 3, sulfate sulfur accounts for 28.53% of total sulfur content in CQDs, organic sulfur compounds (sulfoxide, sulfone, and thiophene) make up 46.15%, and pyrite sulfur constitutes 25.32%. In SCQDs, sulfate sulfur constitutes 10.53% of total sulfur content, organic sulfur compounds make up 76.31%, and pyrite sulfur constitutes 13.16%. Comparatively, SCQDs have a higher proportion of organic sulfur in their sulfur distribution.

Fig. 6(d) displays the full spectra of different catalysts. In CN, elements C and N are present, in CM elements Co, Mo, and O are detected, while in the composite catalyst CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, elements C, N, Co, Mo, and O are observed. This further confirms the successful preparation of the composite catalyst.

Fig. 6(e) presents the C 1s spectra of CN and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The C 1s spectrum of CN can be deconvoluted into N–CN (284.6 eV) and C–C (287.7 eV) peaks [39]. In the C 1s spectrum of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, the peaks corresponding to N–CN and C–C are located at 284.8 eV and 288.2 eV, respectively. These peaks exhibit shifts of 0.2 eV and 0.5 eV towards higher binding energy compared to the C peak in CN.

Fig. 6(f) shows the N 1s spectra of CN and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The N 1s spectrum of CN can be deconvoluted into three peaks: C–N–C (398.2 eV), N-(C)3 (399.7 eV), and C–N–H (400.42 eV) [40]. In the N 1s spectrum of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, the peaks corresponding to C–N–C, N-(C)3, and C–N–H are located at 398.6 eV, 399.9 eV, and 400.8 eV, respectively. These peaks exhibit shifts of 0.4 eV, 0.2 eV, and 0.4 eV towards higher binding energy compared to the peaks in CN.

Fig. 6(g) displays the Co 2p spectra of CM and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The Co 2p spectrum of CM can be deconvoluted into four peaks: Co 2p1/2 (797.4 eV), Co 2p3/2 (781.2 eV), and two satellite peaks at binding energies of 786.4 eV and 803.5 eV [41]. In the Co 2p spectrum of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, the peaks corresponding to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 are located at 797.2 eV and 781.0 eV, respectively, with satellite peaks at binding energies of 786.2 eV and 803.1 eV. These peaks show shifts towards lower binding energy by 0.2 eV for both Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 compared to the peaks in CM.

Fig. 6(h) presents the Mo 3d spectra of CM and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The Mo 3d spectrum of CM can be deconvoluted into two peaks: Mo 3d5/2 (232.5 eV) and Mo 3d3/2 (235.6 eV) [42]. In the Mo 3d spectrum of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, the peaks corresponding to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 are located at 232.3 eV and 235.4 eV, respectively. These peaks exhibit shifts towards lower binding energy by 0.2 eV for both Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 compared to the peaks in CM.

Fig. 6(i) displays the O 1s spectra of CM and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The O 1s spectrum of CM can be deconvoluted into three peaks: lattice O2− (530.4 eV), oxygen vacancies (531.5 eV), and O-related molecules in air (532.9 eV) [43]. In the O 1s spectrum of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, the peaks corresponding to lattice O2−, oxygen vacancies, and O-related molecules in air are located at 530.3 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.7 eV, respectively. These peaks exhibit shifts towards lower binding energy by 0.1 eV, 0.1 eV, and 0.2 eV compared to the peaks in CM. In comparison to CM, the O 1s spectrum of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs shows enhanced peaks related to oxygen vacancies, indicating an increased density of oxygen vacancies that can promote charge carrier separation.

In the XPS spectra of each sample (C spectrum, N spectrum, Co spectrum, Mo spectrum, O spectrum), binding energy changes are observed in the composite sample compared to the individual samples. Specifically, in CN's C and N spectra, the peaks shift towards higher binding energy, indicating electron loss from CN in the composite catalyst. In CM's Co, Mo, and O spectra, the peaks shift towards lower binding energy, indicating electron accumulation in CM in the composite catalyst. This further suggests a strong coupling interaction between CN, CM, and SCQDs, leading to changes in the electron density of C, N, Co, Mo, and O atoms' outer shells. Electrons transfer from CM to CN, creating an inherent electric field from CM to CN, which facilitates the transfer of photogenerated electrons between CN and CM. This also confirms the successful preparation of the composite catalyst CMCN-11/8%SCQDs.

A series of experiments were conducted to investigate the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the prepared catalysts. Of these, TEOA (10%) aqueous solution with pH = 9 was used as a sacrificial reagent when hydrogen generation tests were performed in Fig. 7 (a), (b), (c) and (g). Fig. 7(a) illustrates the hydrogen evolution activity tests for different catalysts. It is evident that the composite of CM and CN, denoted as CMCN, significantly enhances the hydrogen evolution activity. The results indicate that the optimal combination of CM and CN is CMCN-11. To further enhance the hydrogen evolution performance of the samples, catalyst optimization was carried out by incorporating varying proportions of SCQDs onto the CMCN-11 base. Fig. 7(b) displays the hydrogen evolution activity tests for catalysts with different proportions of SCQDs. The introduction of SCQDs enhances the hydrogen evolution amount, with the optimal content of SCQDs being 8%. In other words, the composite photocatalyst CMCN-11/8%SCQDs exhibits the highest hydrogen production efficiency. It was observed that increasing the amount of SCQDs beyond this optimum deteriorates the hydrogen evolution performance, attributed to the aggregation of a large number of SCQDs, which hampers the excitation and transfer of photogenerated electrons [44].

To further explore the enhancement effect of CQDs and SCQDs on the hydrogen evolution activity of CMCN-11, separate hydrogen evolution activity tests were conducted for CMCN-11 composites with CQDs and SCQDs, as shown in Fig. 7(c). After 5 h, the hydrogen evolution amounts for CMCN-11, CMCN-11/8%CQDs, and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs were 78.81 μmol, 120.00 μmol, and 201.55 μmol, respectively. In order to determine the optimal hydrogen evolution environment pH, hydrogen evolution rate tests were performed for CMCN-11/8%SCQDs under different pH conditions, and the results are depicted in Fig. 7(d).

It was found that the optimal pH for hydrogen evolution is pH = 9, achieving a maximum hydrogen evolution rate of 4916.63 μmol g−1 h−1. This is because in the strong alkali hydrogen production environment, the H+ supply capacity is insufficient, resulting in a decrease in the driving force for the reduction of H+ to H2, which reduces the hydrogen evolution activity. When in an acidic environment, relatively high concentrations of H+ will lead to protonation of the solution, thereby affecting the efficiency of the electron donor. Fig. 7(e) presents the AQE (apparent quantum efficiency) test for CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. Different wavelength bandpass filters (420, 475, 500, 520, 550 nm) were used to evaluate the photogenerated quantum yield of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The results indicate that the highest quantum yield is achieved at a wavelength of 420 nm, with a value of 1.78%. Fig. 7(f) presents the results of free radical capture experiments using different reagents. The two abbreviations are isopropanol (IPA) and benzoquinone (BQ). AgNO3 as an electron quencher, TEOA as a hole quencher, IPA (TEOA) as a hydroxyl radical quencher, and BQ (TEOA) as a superoxide radical quencher. From Fig. 7(f), it is evident that the hydrogen evolution amount of CMCN-11/8%CQDs significantly decreases when AgNO3 is used as the electron quencher, indicating the crucial role of photogenerated electrons in the system. Hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals do not exert a significant influence on the hydrogen evolution amount. To test the hydrogen evolution stability of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, hydrogen evolution cycling experiments were conducted, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(g). Four consecutive hydrogen evolution cycles were carried out, revealing that CMCN-11/8%SCQDs maintain a relatively stable hydrogen evolution activity after 20 h of hydrogen evolution reaction, demonstrating good cycling stability. Fig. 7(h) displays the XRD results of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs before and after the reaction. A comparison between the XRD results of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs after 20 h of hydrogen evolution cycling and freshly prepared CMCN-11/8%SCQDs indicates no significant change in relative peak positions and intensities. This suggests that the sample retains its structural stability after 20 h of hydrogen evolution cycling [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL spectroscopy and TRPL spectroscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8 (a) depicts the steady-state fluorescence spectrum. From the fluorescence test results, it is observed that the fluorescence intensity follows the order: EY, SCQDs, CN, CM, CMCN-11, CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. The lowest fluorescence intensity is exhibited by CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, indicating the slowest rate of electron-hole recombination in this case. By comparing the fluorescence intensities of CMCN-11 and CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, it is apparent that the addition of SCQDs effectively mitigates electron-hole recombination [46], preserving more available electrons in the system for hydrogen evolution. CMCN-11/8%SCQDs exhibit the highest carrier separation efficiency [47]. On one hand, this is attributed to the addition of SCQDs acting as electron acceptors, which reduces the band gap distance and induces charge transfer. SCQDs can also store electrons and accelerate the formation of electron-hole pairs. The inclusion of SCQDs introduces new active sites, serving as bridges for efficient electron transportation. On the other hand, this is attributed to the potential formation of heterojunctions between CM and CN, providing favorable conditions for charge transfer [48,49].

Fig. 8(b) displays the transient fluorescence lifetime graph. The relevant parameters were fitted using equations (1) and (2), and Table 4 presents the fluorescence decay parameters for the samples. The average fluorescence lifetime follows the order: EY, SCQDs, CM, CN, CMCN-11, CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. It is noted that the shortest average fluorescence lifetime is exhibited by CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, measuring 0.56 ns, while the average fluorescence lifetime of CMCN-11 is 0.57 ns. The addition of SCQDs is shown to reduce the fluorescence lifetime and enhance the charge separation efficiency [50].

Fig. 9 illustrates the photoelectrochemical testing. Fig. 9(a) presents the transient photocurrent response graph. It is evident from Fig. 9(a) that the photocurrent responses of the samples vary, with CMCN-11/8%SCQDs exhibiting the highest response, followed by CMCN-11. The electron-hole separation efficiency of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs is the highest [51]. The addition of SCQDs effectively enhances the photocurrent response. Fig. 9(b) shows the impedance spectra (EIS), where the EIS curves follow the order of curvature radius from large to small: CN, CM, CMCN-11, CMCN-11/8%SCQDs. This indicates that CMCN-11/8%SCQDs have the smallest charge transfer resistance, best conductivity, and the highest electron transfer efficiency per unit time [52,53], involving more active sites in the hydrogen evolution reaction.

Fig. 9(c) presents the polarization curves (LSV) for the various samples. From Fig. 9(c), it is apparent that CMCN-11/8%SCQDs exhibit a lower overpotential at the same current density, indicating their superior performance in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [54]. This is due to the fact that SCQDs are attached to the surface of CMCN-11, providing more active hydrogen evolution sites, which promote the hydrogen evolution reaction and result in a higher current density. Fig. 9(d) displays the Zeta potentials of CMCN-11 and SCQDs, which are measured to be −42.00 mV and 6.80 mV, respectively. CMCN-11 carries a negative charge on its surface, while SCQDs have a positive charge on their surface. Through electrostatic attraction, the two components are tightly combined [55].

Fig. 10 depicts the UV–visible diffuse reflectance and band gap diagrams of various catalysts, which were analyzed using UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy to investigate their optical properties and band gap characteristics. In Fig. 10(a), the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of different catalysts are presented. Notably, CM exhibits absorption peaks not only in the near-UV region but also in the visible light range of 500 nm–650 nm, demonstrating a broad absorption band. Upon the composite formation of CM and CN, the excellent visible light absorption capability of CM is retained, enhancing the optical absorption range of the composite material. By comparing the optical absorption ranges of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs with CMCN-11, it is evident that the introduction of SCQDs expands the optical absorption range. Utilizing the Kubelka-Munk transformation function [56], the band gap diagrams of CM and CN are derived in figure (b) and (c) respectively, with band gaps of approximately 2.55 eV for CM and 2.68 eV for CN.

Fig. 10(d) and (e) illustrate the Mott-Schottky plots of CM and CN. From the tangents of the Mott-Schottky curves for CM and CN, it can be inferred that both CM and CN belong to n-type semiconductors [57]. Accordingly, the flat band potentials (Efb) for CM and CN are determined as −0.87 eV and −1.07 eV, respectively. For n-type semiconductors, the energy level of the conduction band (ECB) is generally around 0.1 eV–0.2 eV lower than the flat band potential (Efb) [58,59]. Consequently, in comparison to the saturated calomel electrode (SCE), the ECB of CM and CN is estimated at approximately −1.07 eV and −1.27 eV. Moreover, considering the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE), the ECB values for CM and CN are −0.83 eV and −1.03 eV respectively (ENHE = ESCE + 0.24 eV) [60]. Given the previously established band gap values of approximately 2.55 eV for CM and 2.68 eV for CN, their valence band positions (VB) can be calculated using the equation EVB = ECB + Eg, yielding VB values of 1.72 eV for CM and 1.65 eV for CN.

To verify the calculated band gap results, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was employed to ascertain the band gaps of CM and CN. Fig. 10(f) presents the UPS spectra for CM and CN, indicating UPS widths of 15.06 eV and 15.13 eV respectively. By subtracting the excitation energy (21.22 eV) from the UPS width, the valence band positions (EVB) relative to vacuum are determined [61]. Accordingly, the EVB values for CM and CN are calculated as 6.16 eV and 6.09 eV respectively. By relating the standard hydrogen electrode potential (Ee) to the vacuum energy level (Eabs) using the equation (Ee = Eabs-4.44), the EVB values for CM and CN are determined to be 1.72 vs. NHE and 1.65 vs. NHE respectively. Importantly, the band gap values obtained through UPS calculations are consistent with those derived from the Mott-Schottky curve method.

Fig. 11 illustrates the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution mechanism of the CMCN-11/8%SCQDs system. Based on the band gap analysis of CM and CN, it is evident that their energy band structures overlap. Both CM and CN are n-type semiconductors. When CM and CN come into contact, the Fermi level shifts, causing band bending. Thus, it is speculated that CM and CN form an S-type heterojunction structure [62–64]. Due to the overlapping energy band structures of CM and CN, electrons in the conduction band of CN spontaneously transfer to the valence band of CM [65]. The introduction of SCQDs increases the charge density between CM and CN, thereby generating an internal electric field within CM, CN, and SCQDs. At the interface between CM and CN, two regions are formed: an electron accumulation layer and an electron depletion layer. This arrangement creates an intrinsic electric field E pointing from CM to CN, which provides favorable conditions for efficient electron transfer [66]. CMCN-11/8%SCQDs are nanomaterials capable of adsorbing dye molecules, and SCQDs broaden the light absorption range [67]. Under light excitation, dye molecules on CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, such as EY, will be converted to excited states EY1 ∗ and further to EY3 ∗ [68,69]. In the presence of triethanolamine (TEOA), EY3 ∗ is converted to EY− while simultaneously oxidizing TEOA to TEOA+ [70,71]. Assisted by SCQDs, EY− transfers electrons to the surface of CMCN-11/8%SCQDs, thus completing the hydrogen evolution process. SCQDs exhibit excellent electron transfer ability during the reaction and act as bridges for electron transfer [72]. They function both as electron acceptors and donors, effectively impeding the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing their separation efficiency [73,74], thus facilitating efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

In this study, high-sulfur coal was selected as the carbon source, and SCQDs were successfully synthesized using a one-step green method with ultrasound-assisted hydrogen peroxide oxidation. These SCQDs were employed to assist the photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen evolution in the presence of CM and CN. The results demonstrate that the novel composite photocatalyst CMCN-11/8%SCQDs exhibits remarkable hydrogen evolution activity, achieving a maximum hydrogen evolution rate of 4916.63 μmol g−1 h−1 at pH 9. The AQE reached 1.78% at a wavelength of 420 nm. The CN nanosheets in the CMCN-11/8%SCQDs composite catalyst contribute to a large specific surface area, and the attachment of SCQDs onto the CMCN-11 surface provides additional active sites for hydrogen evolution. SCQDs possess excellent electron transfer capability, serving as bridges for electron transfer. They act as both electron acceptors and donors, effectively inhibiting the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing their separation efficiency. This study successfully transforms low-cost coal into environmentally friendly and high-value carbon quantum dots, which are utilized as co-catalysts for photocatalytic water splitting and hydrogen evolution. It provides a promising direction for the deep processing and functional applications of coal.

Shiyong Xu and Mei Li conceived and designed the experiments. Shiyong Xu conducted the experiments. Mei Li zhiliang Jin contributed reagents/materials/analysis tools. Yijun Wang assisted with data analysis and processing. Shiyong Xu and Mei Li co-wrote the initial draft. Zhiliang Jin revised the manuscript.
